speaker: interviewer
text: Ben, can you describe this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, this is my sister who was one year older than I-- than I was. This picture was taken approximately 1939. That would make her approximately 15 years of age. She did enter into concentration camp in Auschwitz. She was chosen to be one of the workers. She never survived. I have no idea what happened to her. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I guess I didn't want to know. I heard some rumors, the way she passed away, and I just tried to dismiss it. And, however, I will from now on. There are some records which the Germans have kept. And I do want to know how she died. Her name was Ester. She was beautiful, she was pretty, and she was very smart also. She was a mathematician at a young age.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you describe this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, this is my sister. She was the oldest one in the family. Her name is Rachel, Rochelle, and my brother. This picture undoubtedly was taken in the same city as my-- as the picture previously done with Ester. This picture could have been taken approximately in 1930, 1933, or '34. This is the only photograph I have of our older sister. There are no other pictures left anywheres. She had two children. And she, of course, was-- went straight-- she wouldn't part with her kids. And she was taken away in the trucks straight to the gas chambers. Her husband was also killed during the war. He survived almost the entire war. But in the last bombing raids when Auschwitz was bombed, a stray bomb, I guess, found the camp, and he was killed in one of those raids. So this is the only picture I have of her. My brother, you have seen him. I mean, I talked about him before. He lived in Costa Rico after the war. But this picture was taken again, as I said, in our city where we lived, Hajnowka. And it looks like he is in here about [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 18, 19 years of age, maybe 20. Let's see. I was-- No, I was right-- about 20 years of age. He was born in 1914.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you describe this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, this photograph was taken towards the end of April or beginning of May, 1945, right after our liberation. I was in a little town called [NON-ENGLISH] where I was looking for my friend from Belgium. And I was told by some other Polish people that they saw Jewish women in a town about, I believe it was, 12 kilometers or 18 kilometers away. And I thought that there were-- everybody was killed. There were no Jewish women, especially, left anymore in this world. And I figured that since we were the only few that ran away, that we were the only survivors in the whole world. We didn't see any other Jewish refugees with us-- about a dozen of us were.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when I found out, I grabbed a bicycle. And I bicycled the 12 or 18 miles to that city. They told me where it was. It was Schwarzwald, which means Black Forest. And it was the Black Hotel, Black Adler Hotel, Schwarz Adler.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went to that hotel. They told me this is where they saw the girls. And they were still wearing concentration camp uniforms except they were clean and cleaned up. And the guy on the right, of course, first row, standing up there, that's me. You can see probably I have very little hair. It was just right after the liberation, so probably my head wasn't shaved since about April 1 or so.

speaker: interviewee
text: So and right next to me is my brother. And the girl on my left, the first girl standing up-- actually on my right right now-- she didn't want to stay anymore in that hotel. When she saw that there were still Jewish men left, she saw some people in there which were-- she thought they were Nazis. She thought that she recognized them. So she came with me back to the city where I was. And I carried her on the frame for 12 miles or 18 miles. She was riding with me. She didn't want to spend one more day in that city.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then the girls-- a day or two later, the girls came back. And they joined us in that particular city. I have no idea what happened to them. I don't even remember the names. I wish to God I would have put down the names in there. But those were the first four Jewish women that I have seen after being liberated.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who took the photograph, Ben?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was an American soldier who took this picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you describe this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This photo was taken in Brussels in 1946 just about the time before we left. On the right, that's me with a lot of hair already. On the left is my brother. In the middle is David Stern. This is the gentleman who was responsible for talking us in-- coming to the-- coming to Belgium after the war. And he was our host for the time that we were there. And this story, of course, is on tape now. So you'll know who this man is. The final story of him is since he was, I guess, a socialist, I understand in later years he has left Belgium, went to Hungary, and that's when I have lost complete contact with him once he went into the communist Hungary at the time. So I don't know what happened to him. And that would make him today approximately [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 85 years old or so, so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ben, can you describe this document?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, this document was obtained in the Auschwitz Museum in 1976. The people that I lived with went back to their place of birth, which was Kosava. The city of Kosava were being there. There was Shirley and Jack [? Heiden. ?] Shirley was a journalist for the And so while being in Poland, of course, they visited the-- the Auschwitz camp. And they asked-- since I lived with them, they knew all about me. So they walked in, and they asked do they have any documents, giving them my name and my brother's name. So they asked them if they had my number. And they said, no, they don't know my number. They said it purposely.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they went in, and they got the documents. And the document states that I was a prisoner in concentration camp of Birkenau-Auschwitz and Buna, whatever it says in there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And today I have arrived, and-- just a document that I was in the camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you describe this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This photo was taken just before my graduation, approximately 1951. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was a young man then and proud to be here. And that was the year I graduated.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ben, would you describe this photo please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This photo is the love of my life. We were engaged. This is the engagement picture. Her name is Ester. She was the most beautiful girl that I knew. And she remained like this all through the years.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this taken about?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in 1952, sometimes in February or so, January or February. Since we got engaged January 18, so it was taken right after this.

speaker: interviewer
text: And would you describe this photo please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's one of the happiest days of my life, when we got married. This picture was taken October 26, 1952. And this is after the ceremony. We were just entering, I think, the hall for the reception, the reception hall. And as you can see, I'm happy and so was she.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ben, please describe this photo.

speaker: interviewee
text: This photo was taken in 1993 at a cousin's wedding. In-- in my eyes, she has never changed. She looks still as of the day we got engaged. She was really beautiful. That is--

speaker: interviewer
text: Ben, please describe this photo.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, this photo-- I'll be describing from left to right. This was taken on October-- oh, I better not make a mistake in there-- October 10, my youngest daughter's wedding, which took place because the wedding wasn't here-- the ceremony-- but the reception was at Beth Abraham. From the left, my daughter Dina, whom you'll meet in a few minutes. I'm standing next to her. Right next to me is the bride. That's Faye and, of course, her husband, Mark. My wife is next to Mark. And my son Larry is next to Mom. And now, we'll go back to the left again. That's my grandson Shay and to the right, my granddaughter Shanna, which you'll all meet in a few minutes. And this was really a great moment in our life. This was the youngest one getting married, and-- although my son isn't married yet. But he will be soon. And it was a happy, happy time in our lives when my wife was still healthy, and we did not know for many problems.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that taken then?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in 1993.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ben, would you please describe your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I'll start with the grandchildren. This is Shanna. She's 12 years old, and she's beautiful. My grandson Shay-- did I say she's my granddaughter? OK. This is Shay. He's 13 years old, going on 15. My daughter Dina-- she is the second child in the family and their mother. This is my son, Larry. I only have one son. This is my daughter Faye. This is the youngest one. And right next to her is Mark. That's her husband. They were recently married. We saw them on the previous pictures in there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is our latest addition, Sarah. And she is one year old and-- one and a half? One and a half years old. I remember the birthday. She was born February 23, '95.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is our family. And unfortunately, of course, there's one missing. Their mother is missing in here. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] But we are all glad to be here. I made that video, number one, because she was on my case. And she was right, too, because I have really never spoken to my kids about the Holocaust, never spoken about my family. I think it will be easier now, not just because there's a video, which Ida said I won't get for nine weeks-- for nine months. But it will be easier for me to talk now about it since I already really went through this with you now. And at least they will know more about my life in Europe and here with my schooling and about my life in here-- more than what they knew up till now. And, of course, the video was not just made for them, and I hope they can learn from it. The video was also made for-- for history. As I indicated before, we, the survivors, they are far and few between now. They are-- it's a breed which is-- which is almost extinct. It's more than 50 years after the war now. And we were close-mouthed up till now. We weren't talking because, some indicated before, we just couldn't believe ourselves that something like this could happen. So how could we make-- tell other people and make them believe what we went through.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we are more [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] open-minded now and more, I guess, at ease to talk about it. And I hope that maybe future generations can learn from it-- that's something what happened should never, never, never happen again. Dina, would you like to say something for the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I would. Just as it's important to preserve the history of the survivors and with these tapes not only will the continuity of all of the survivors remain, but it's just as important to preserve our own family's history and for my brother and sister and my brother-in-law and my-- and my children and my niece, we'll be able to keep this and keep it in our hearts and our minds. And hopefully, we'll be able to convey to the rest of the world that in spite of the fact that this horrible, horrible monstrous thing happened, that we are here. And we are living testament to the fact that we are all survivors. And we are a product of this one survivor, the most important to us all, our dad, and our grandfather, our zayde.

speaker: interviewee
text: Great.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the conclusion of the video-taping session. We used six tapes.
